summary: This lecture provides a comprehensive Islamic jurisprudential analysis of fasting during Ramadan, focusing on the criteria for fasting, conditions that permit breaking the fast, and the pillars of fasting. The speaker explains five conditions for obligatory fasting (Islam, puberty, sanity, residency, and health) and discusses three types of illnesses that permit breaking the fast. The lecture then details the two pillars of fasting: intention (which must be made before dawn) and refraining from five specific things that break the fast (eating/drinking, self-induced vomiting, sexual intercourse, and menstruation/postpartum bleeding for women). The second portion emphasizes that while fasting is a key component of Ramadan, the most prominent feature of the month is that it was when the Quran was revealed, and fasting serves as preparation for engaging deeply with Quranic recitation and reflection.
suggested_title: Conditions and Pillars of Fasting in Ramadan: Islamic Jurisprudence and the Centrality of Quran
